title: Coil-built wall-pocket planter
subtitle: A flat-backed air-dry clay planter that hangs on a wall
excerpt: A coil-built pocket planter with a flat back for hanging, a rounded front, and drainage holes at the base. Built in air-dry clay with a hanger loop added before drying. Sized for a small succulent or herb seedling in a terracotta pot that drops inside.
sourceNotes: Wall-hung pocket planters appear in earthenware traditions across Mediterranean and South American pottery. Technique derived from first principles. Flat-back shaping informed by traditional wall-hung water vessels from the V&A open-access collection.

glossaryTerms.definition: A rope of clay rolled between the palms or on the board, used as the building unit in coil construction.
Scratching joining surfaces and brushing them with clay slurry before pressing together.
The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to join and trim.

content.text: A wall-pocket planter is a coil-built form with a flat back and a rounded front, sized to hold a small pot. The flat back sits flush against the wall; a clay loop fired into the rim at the back provides the hanging point. No kiln is needed: air-dry clay sets at room temperature. The finished planter is not waterproof, so a small inner pot provides the actual growing environment.

items.name: Small succulent in pot to test fit

content.text: Wedge 400 g of clay until the surface is smooth and even. Set aside 30 g for the hanging loop and patch clay. Prepare slurry by working a pea-sized piece of clay with a few drops of water until it reaches a thick paste consistency.
Press a small slab by hand to a roughly oval shape, 90 mm x 70 mm and 8 mm thick. This is the base. Place it flat on the board. Pierce two small drainage holes through it with the needle tool.
Roll a rope of clay between the palms until it is 10 mm thick and about 30 cm long. This is one

the base edge and press the first coil around the oval perimeter. Smooth the inside join with a finger, pulling clay from the coil down into the base. Build upward, scoring and slipping between each coil, until the planter reaches 120 mm high (about eight coils).

While the walls are still soft, press the back of the form flat against the board with the wooden rib. Work from the base up, applying firm even pressure. The front remains rounded; the back becomes a flat face 90 mm wide. Let the form stiffen for 30 minutes before adding the hanger.

, roll the reserved clay into a short rope 8 mm thick and 80 mm long. Bend it into a loop. Score and slip both ends and two matching points on the flat back face, 20 mm down from the rim. Press the loop ends firmly into the marked points. Reinforce each join by pressing a small collar of fresh clay around the base of each end and smoothing it flat with the rib.

Work over the outside surface with the wooden rib, pressing down across the coil seams to merge them. The coil texture can be left showing on the rounded front if preferred. The flat back should be smoothed completely so it sits flush against a wall.

Dry under a loose plastic tent for 24 hours, then allow to dry fully over two to three days. Paint with acrylics and seal with two coats of PVA varnish. The drainage holes must remain unblocked. Hang using a screw or cup hook; insert a small plastic pot before adding the plant so no compost or water contacts the clay directly.
Make a trio of progressively smaller planters to hang in a vertical row.
Press a leaf or fern into the outside wall before smoothing for a botanical impression.
Build the form deeper to hold a standard 9 cm pot. Increase the base oval to 110 mm x 90 mm and add two more coil rows.

items.symptom: Hanger loop falls off during drying
items.cause: Loop was attached to wet clay, or join was not scored deeply enough
items.fix: Always attach the loop at leather-hard. Score both surfaces deeply, use thick slurry, and reinforce the base of each end with a fresh clay collar

items.symptom: Flat back curves away from the wall as it dries
items.cause: The clay dried unevenly; front dried faster than the back
items.fix: Prop the flat back against a flat board while drying, or tape a flat piece of cardboard against it for the first day

items.cause: Scoring and slipping was insufficient between coils
items.fix: Fill the crack with fresh slurry and press a thin clay strip over it. Score the strip and surrounding surface before applying
